stem: A function \( T \) on the nonnegative integers is defined by \( T(0) = [[x0]] \) and \( T(k) = [[a]]\,T(k-1) + ([[b]]) \) for \( k \ge 1 \). What is \( T([[n]]) \)?
solution: Apply the rule repeatedly from \( T(0) = [[x0]] \): each step multiplies by [[a]] and adds ([[b]]). After [[n]] steps, \( T([[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies only the multiplicative part and drops the added constant at every step.
This adds the constant just once instead of at each of the [[n]] steps.
This performs only a single step, computing \( T(1) \) instead of \( T([[n]]) \).
This is off by the constant [[b]] from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A function \( T \) on the nonnegative integers is defined by \( T(0) = [[x0]] \) and \( T(k) = T(k-1)^{2} + ([[c]]) \) for \( k \ge 1 \). What is \( T([[n]]) \)?
solution: Apply the rule repeatedly from \( T(0) = [[x0]] \), squaring the previous value and adding ([[c]]) each time. After [[n]] steps, \( T([[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes only \( T(1) \) instead of continuing to \( T([[n]]) \).
This squares the whole expression an extra time rather than following the recursive rule step by step.
This confuses repeated squaring with a single larger exponent on the initial value.
This adds the constant [[n]] times to a single squaring instead of re-squaring at every step.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A function \( T \) on the nonnegative integers is defined by \( T(0) = [[x0]] \) and \( T(k) = T(k-1) + k \) for \( k \ge 1 \). What is \( T([[n]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This adds \( k \) only once, at the final step, instead of accumulating it at every step from 1 to [[n]].
This multiplies the initial value into the sum instead of just adding it once at the start.
This drops the initial value \( T(0) = [[x0]] \) entirely.
This sums only \( 1 \) through \( [[n]]-1 \), stopping the accumulation one step early.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A function \( T \) on the positive integers is defined by \( T(0) = [[x0]] \) and \( T(k) = k \cdot T(k-1) \) for \( k \ge 1 \). What is \( T([[n]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by the final index [[n]] only once, instead of by every index from 1 to [[n]].
This drops the initial value \( T(0) = [[x0]] \) entirely.
This stops the multiplication one step early, omitting the final factor of [[n]].
This confuses the repeated multiplication by increasing indices with a fixed power of [[n]].
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A function \( T \) on the nonnegative integers is defined by \( T(0) = [[x0]] \) and \( T(k) = \left\lfloor T(k-1)/2 \right\rfloor \) for \( k \ge 1 \). What is \( T([[n]]) \)?
solution: Each step halves the value and rounds down; applying this [[n]] times to \( T(0) = [[x0]] \) gives \( T([[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This performs only a single halving, computing \( T(1) \) instead of \( T([[n]]) \).
This rounds up at the end instead of rounding down after every step.
This leaves the final value as a raw (non-integer) fraction instead of the correctly rounded-down result.
This confuses repeated halving with subtracting [[n]] once.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
